topic: Two friends realize they remember a shared event completely differently
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the chipped paint on the windowsill, the afternoon sun doing little to warm the chill settling in his chest. Maya was recounting the evening of the disastrous camping trip, her voice bright and animated, detailing the sudden downpour and the ridiculous scare with the raccoon. He nodded along, his gaze fixed on a dust mote dancing in the light beam. He felt a dull, heavy weight in his limbs, as if gravity had increased tenfold. When she finished, he offered a small, noncommittal hum. "Right," he said, pushing himself up from the worn armchair. "That sounds... eventful." He didn't elaborate. He simply moved toward the kitchen, the conversation already receding into the background noise of his own quiet processing.

[story 2]

"I still can't believe we actually did that," Maya sighed, running a hand through her already messy hair. She was recounting the night they spent at the old abandoned warehouse, the tension, the adrenaline. Across the room, Alex watched her, his expression perfectly smooth, his eyes tracking the movement of a distant shadow. He took a slow sip of his lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug feeling strangely inert in his hand. "Yeah," he replied, his tone even, almost flat. "It was... memorable." He didn't lean in, didn't offer any added color to the memory she was painting. He just watched the steam rise from his cup, letting the silence stretch between them like an unyielding fabric.

[story 3]

Sarah leaned against the brick wall, the city noise a constant, low thrum she barely registered. Mark was describing the chaotic street festival from last summer—the crowds, the smells, the sheer sensory overload. She listened, her posture relaxed to the point of near collapse, her eyes unfocused, looking past the vibrant chaos. When he paused for breath, she offered a barely audible exhale. "Mmm-hmm," she murmured, her voice sounding distant, as if speaking from another room. She didn't offer an anecdote, didn't ask a follow-up question. She simply let the description wash over her, a spectator observing a scene she wasn't truly participating in.
